Renaissance & Late Scholasticism

John of the Cross

1542 – 1591

Dark night, contemplation, and the soul’s ascent beyond images.

St. John of the Cross does not offer a “philosophy of mind” in the modern analytic sense. He offers something broader and, in some respects, more demanding: a metaphysical, ascetical, phenomenological, and theological psychology of the human person ordered toward union with God. A philosophy of mind can be extracted from it, but only if “mind” is not narrowed to consciousness, cognition, representation, or brain process. For John, the relevant subject is the alma, the soul-person, considered through its lower and higher operations: sense, imagination, appetite, understanding, memory, will, spirit, and the deeper “substance” or center of the soul.

The central thesis I would extract is this: the human mind is an embodied, affectively governed, faculty-structured soul whose natural cognition begins through sense and image, but whose final perfection lies not in representational mastery but in loving, participatory transformation by God. Its highest cognition is therefore not discursive possession of an object, but dark, loving, receptive union with a reality that exceeds every created mode of knowing. That makes John’s philosophy of mind simultaneously empirical, apophatic, affective, virtue-theoretic, anti-possessive, and theocentric.

John’s remarks on God and angels are not peripheral. They are structurally necessary. God is not merely the “object” of mystical cognition; God is the infinite Wisdom and Love who actively purifies, darkens, illuminates, and transforms the soul’s cognitive and affective powers. Angels, likewise, are not ornamental figures. They function as examples of non-embodied spiritual cognition, as mediators of illumination, and as limits: even angelic minds remain finite before God, and the highest union of the soul with God surpasses angelic mediation.

1. Textual and conceptual frame

The four prose works that matter most for this reconstruction are The Ascent of Mount Carmel, The Dark Night, The Spiritual Canticle, and The Living Flame of Love. The traditional ordering is important. The Ascent analyzes the active purification of sense and spirit; the Dark Night analyzes passive purification by God; the Spiritual Canticle describes the soul’s search, encounter, and spiritual marriage; the Living Flame describes the most intimate transformed state of the soul in divine love. Peers’s general introduction to the English translation also emphasizes John’s Aristotelian-Thomistic background, his use of Scripture, scholastic theology, Pseudo-Dionysius, Augustine, Gregory, and other authorities, and his striking psychological acuity.

A modern reader must resist two reductions. The first is to read John as merely “devotional,” as if his analysis of faculties, memory, understanding, appetite, and affect had no philosophical content. The second is to secularize him too quickly, as if “God,” “faith,” “angels,” and “grace” were dispensable metaphors for inner psychological states. In John, those realities do real explanatory work. The divine self-communication is not an optional religious overlay on an otherwise autonomous psychology; it is the decisive agent in the transformation of the mind.

The Spanish-language philosophical reception is helpful here because it often takes John’s anthropology more seriously as anthropology. Lucero González Suárez, for example, describes John’s notion of alma as equivalent in important respects to “human being” or “man,” and reads his distinction between lower and higher parts of the soul through the different experiences and operations each makes possible. She also emphasizes the external and internal senses and the higher operations of understanding, will, and memory in relation to loving union with God.

2. The architecture of the human soul-mind

John’s basic psychology is not Cartesian. He does not begin with an isolated thinking subject whose essential feature is self-transparent consciousness. His anthropology is closer to a classical Christian faculty psychology: the human being is a soul-body unity whose soul has lower and higher operations. The lower part is bound to sense, imagination, appetite, and passion; the higher or spiritual part includes understanding, memory, and will. The “mind,” if we use that word, is distributed across these operations rather than confined to one inner theater.

John explicitly describes the journey to union as a passage through nights. The first night concerns the sensual part; the second concerns the spiritual part, and the second is “darker” and more severe. This distinction matters philosophically. The lower part is not evil as such; it is the embodied and affective basis of ordinary human life. But it can dominate the person through disordered appetite. The higher part is not automatically pure; understanding, memory, and will also require purification.

The first major cognitive principle in John is an empiricist one, though embedded in scholastic metaphysics rather than modern empiricism. He says the soul, when infused into the body, is like a blank board and naturally knows through the senses, which are like windows. This is crucial: John does not treat ordinary cognition as pure inward illumination. Natural knowing is embodied, mediated, and image-bearing. External senses supply material; internal senses, especially imagination and fancy, form and retain images; the understanding works upon what is presented; memory retains; will responds affectively and electively.

The Ascent makes this architecture explicit when it says the understanding receives knowledge through natural and supernatural channels. Natural knowledge includes what the understanding can grasp through bodily senses or its own power; supernatural knowledge exceeds natural ability. John further distinguishes corporeal supernatural knowledge received through outward or inward bodily senses from spiritual supernatural knowledge. This is not a crude anti-intellectualism. It is a layered account of cognitive access.

The three higher powers are understanding, memory, and will. John repeatedly maps them onto the three theological virtues: faith purifies and transforms understanding, hope purifies and transforms memory, and charity purifies and transforms will. In the Ascent, he states that the understanding must be emptied by faith, memory by hope, and will by charity, because the soul cannot be united with God by ordinary understanding, enjoyment, imagination, or sense. The Spiritual Canticle gives the same structure in another key: the soul languishes in understanding because it does not see God, suffers in will because it does not possess God as delight, and dies in memory because it remembers its privation of perfect fruition.

The status of memory is especially important. In much scholastic psychology, memory can be treated as a function of intellect rather than as an autonomous rational power. Spanish scholarship has debated whether John innovates by treating memory as a third rational faculty alongside understanding and will, or whether his language can be reconciled with the common scholastic position. Elizabeth Wilhelmsen notes that this debate remains unresolved in the secondary literature and frames the key question as whether memory is really distinct from intellect or only a function of it. For a philosophy of mind extracted from John, this is not a minor technicality. Memory, for John, is not merely storage; it is the faculty of possession, temporal self-relation, recollection, imagined security, and vulnerability to attachment.

3. Natural cognition: sense, image, representation, and proportion

John’s natural epistemology is representational in the classical sense: the mind knows creatures through forms, images, figures, concepts, and apprehensions. The senses and imagination provide the soul with cognitive material. The understanding can then reason, meditate, compare, and infer. This ordinary structure is indispensable for beginners, for moral life, and for practical governance. John’s critique is not that these operations are false. It is that they are not proportionate to God as the final term of union.

The philosophical principle is proportionality. A means must be proportionate to its end. No creaturely form, image, concept, or apprehension is proportionate to God because God is infinite and creatures are finite. John therefore argues that no creature, no natural knowledge, and not even the finite excellence of angels or saints can serve as the proximate means of union with God. The proximate means must be faith, because faith relates the understanding to God precisely under obscurity rather than under a finite conceptual likeness.

This gives John a distinctive anti-representational thesis about the highest cognition. It is not that representation is useless. It is that representation cannot be ultimate. Forms and images can point, prepare, discipline, and inflame; they cannot contain or proportionately mediate the divine essence. Hence John’s famous insistence that the soul approaches God more by not understanding than by trying to understand in its ordinary mode.

This anti-representationalism must not be exaggerated into iconoclasm or contempt for ordinary religious practice. John distinguishes the use of images from attachment to images. In Ascent III he warns that images, devotions, places, and ceremonies can become occasions of vanity or possessive attachment, but he does not deny their legitimate role in recollection and devotion when they are subordinated to God. His position is therefore not “images are bad,” but “images become spiritually and cognitively dangerous when the will clings to them as though they were God or as though affective satisfaction were union.”

4. Appetite, affect, and the will as governor of the soul

John’s philosophy of mind is deeply affective. The mind is not purified by better information alone. It is distorted by love, fear, desire, grief, hope, delight, and attachment. What a person wants shapes what the person can attend to, judge, remember, imagine, and endure.

This is why the first night concerns appetite. John says it is not created things themselves that damage the soul, but the will’s desire for them. Disordered desire occupies the soul, torments it, darkens it, and binds it. The will is therefore not a merely executive faculty added after cognition. It governs the passions and draws the whole soul toward or away from its object.

In Ascent III, John identifies joy, hope, grief, and fear as passions governed by the will and says that the strength of the soul lies in its faculties, passions, and desires being rightly governed by the will. The way to union consists in purging the will of disordered affections so that it becomes conformed to the divine will. A French Carmelite summary captures this point well: John’s teaching aims at union of the soul with God by love, and the journey requires the ordering of fantasy, imagination, the irascible and concupiscible powers, the three higher powers, and the passions of joy, hope, sorrow, and fear.

For philosophy of mind, this is a major thesis: cognition is affectively conditioned. Disordered love produces cognitive distortion. Purified love produces clearer judgment. John even says that emptying memory produces tranquility, peace of mind, purity of conscience, preparedness for wisdom, and truer judgment because the soul is no longer disturbed by possessive recollection.

This anticipates, without being reducible to, modern concerns about attention, affective salience, cognitive bias, and embodied emotion. The will is not simply “choice”; it is the affective-intentional orientation of the person. The will determines what the mind treats as valuable, threatening, desirable, or unbearable.

5. Faith, hope, and charity as transformations of mind

The theological virtues are not merely moral ornaments in John. They are the principal transformative operators of the mind.

Faith transforms understanding. It does not abolish intelligence; it detaches understanding from the demand that God be possessed by clear natural concepts. John says faith is dark to the understanding because it concerns what exceeds natural light. But this darkness is not ignorance in the ordinary sense. It is a mode of contact with a reality too luminous for natural comprehension. The same divine light that illumines also darkens when it exceeds the capacity of the faculty.

Hope transforms memory. Memory clings to possessions, images, past satisfactions, injuries, securities, and possible futures. Hope empties memory not because the past is meaningless, but because the soul must not possess itself through recollection. In John, hope is anti-possessive. It opens memory toward what it does not yet possess: God as incomprehensible and final good. In Ascent III, he says memory must be voided of natural apprehensions to attain union, not so that memory is destroyed, but so that it can be absorbed beyond particular forms in hope toward God.

Charity transforms will. Charity does not merely add benevolent feeling; it conforms the will to God’s will. Since the will governs passions and desires, charity reorganizes the whole affective economy of the person. The final state is not detached neutrality but intensified love. In the Living Flame, John describes the transformed will as moved by the Holy Spirit, so that its acts of love are made one with the divine flame.

Italian reception, especially through the line from Agamben discussed in a recent OpenEdition article, stresses a paradox that is genuinely important: for John, the theological virtues function not only as positive edificatory virtues but also as powers of obscuration, negation, and dispossession. Faith darkens understanding, hope empties memory, and charity strips the will of possessive affection. This is a strong interpretive insight, provided one adds that the negation is ordered toward transformation, not nihilism.

6. Meditation, contemplation, and non-discursive knowledge

John distinguishes discursive meditation from infused contemplation. Discursive meditation uses concepts, reasoning, images, comparison, and affective acts. It is necessary and good at the right stage. But at a certain point, the soul may be called beyond it. John gives signs for this transition: the person can no longer meditate or reason as before, has no desire to fix sense or imagination on other things, and experiences a loving, peaceful attentiveness to God without distinct discursive acts of memory, understanding, or will.

This “loving attentiveness” is central to John’s philosophy of mind. It is not blankness. It is not self-hypnosis. It is not the suppression of cognition into vacancy. It is a non-discursive, affectively charged, receptive awareness whose object is not grasped as a finite representation. John is careful not to confuse this with melancholy, incapacity, or pathological dullness; he explicitly warns that inability to meditate can arise from temperament or disturbance rather than contemplative transition.

The Dark Night identifies infused contemplation as an inflowing of God into the soul that purges ignorance and imperfection. The soul is taught secretly, without fully understanding how. This divine wisdom is dark because it exceeds the soul’s natural capacity and because the soul’s impurity makes the encounter painful. The same wisdom that purges also illumines. Here John’s epistemology becomes apophatic: the highest light appears as darkness, not because God is unintelligible in Himself, but because God is too intelligible for finite modes of comprehension.

This creates a distinctive kind of mystical epistemology. Knowing is not limited to proposition, image, or inference. There is a “general, loving knowledge” of God that is not reducible to a sensory image, an abstract concept, or a discursive conclusion. But John does not permit private experience to become arbitrary authority. Mystical cognition must be purified, tested, subordinated to faith, and guarded from curiosity.

7. Memory, selfhood, and holy dispossession

The philosophy of memory in John deserves special attention. Memory is not simply a mental archive. It is a mode of self-possession. Through memory, the person holds images, experiences, injuries, consolations, plans, and spiritual achievements. Because of this, memory can become a powerful obstacle to union. It gives the soul material for rumination, comparison, fear, nostalgia, self-importance, and false security.

John’s demand that memory be emptied can sound psychologically destructive, but he explicitly rejects that interpretation. In Ascent III, he anticipates the objection that voiding memory would destroy natural faculties and replies that God perfects nature. Once the habit of union is attained, natural and moral actions become more perfect, not less. The faculties are not annihilated but moved in a new way by God.

This is one of the most important points for extracting a philosophy of mind: purification is not elimination. John is not advocating cognitive lobotomy, repression, or permanent dissociation. He is describing the removal of possessive attachment from memory so that memory can function more truthfully and freely. The soul may become less dominated by intrusive recollections and more available to what charity and practical duty require. In the same passage, John even notes that divine touches can disturb the “brain where memory has its seat,” causing oblivion or suspension of imagination, especially in beginnings. This is striking because he can acknowledge a bodily seat or correlate of memory while still treating memory as a spiritual faculty involved in union with God.

A Spanish article on the “privation of memories” during the dark night argues similarly that John does not treat memory as an entity but as a natural power capable of sensitive, intellectual, and spiritual operations. That reading is philosophically fruitful: memory is multilevel. It operates in embodied images, intellectual recollection, and spiritual orientation toward possession or dispossession.

8. The dark night as first-person opacity

One of John’s most philosophically interesting claims is that advanced spiritual life can involve not greater first-person clarity but greater first-person opacity. In the dark night, a person may feel abandoned, faithless, dry, incapable, and spiritually dead precisely while being purified and drawn more deeply into union.

A contemporary Spanish-language article by Begoña Pessis García and José Tomás Alvarado Marambio uses the dark night to challenge internalist theories of faith. They argue that the dark night is difficult to explain if faith must be internally transparent to the subject. In the dark night, the believer may have faith without knowing that he or she has faith, or may be unable to access the epistemic value of that faith from the first-person perspective. They conclude that cognitivist and externalist accounts of faith are better suited to the phenomenon.

This is highly relevant to philosophy of mind. John’s dark night undermines any simplistic identification of mental reality with what is introspectively clear. The soul’s deepest state may not be fully available to reflective self-awareness. A person can be undergoing transformation without being able to identify it as transformation. This is not merely “unconscious process” in a modern psychological sense, though there may be analogies. It is theologically grounded first-person opacity: God’s action exceeds the subject’s self-interpretive powers.

9. Supernatural apprehensions, private knowledge, and discernment

John has a nuanced account of extraordinary cognition: visions, locutions, intuitions, touches, prophetic impressions, and knowledge of persons or things. He neither flatly denies them nor encourages fascination with them. His rule is severe: the soul should not seek them, cling to them, or use them as the proximate means to union.

In Ascent II, he describes “naked truths” or passive intuitions of divine things that are ineffable and cannot be produced by the soul’s own effort. Some concern God’s attributes or divine realities; others may concern lower truths, persons, or events. But he repeatedly warns that such experiences must be subordinated to faith, renounced as objects of attachment, and submitted to discernment.

John is especially important on the knowledge of other minds. He states that spiritual persons cannot naturally know the thoughts or inward states of others. They may infer from signs, or they may receive supernatural illumination, but such judgments can be mistaken and must be handled carefully. This gives John a clear view of mental privacy: ordinary access to another person’s mind is mediated by signs and interpretation. Claims to direct access require supernatural explanation and are epistemically dangerous.

John’s warnings against curiosity are also philosophically significant. He argues that seeking supernatural knowledge beyond the limits God has ordained for natural governance is wrong and spiritually dangerous. Even if an angelic or supernatural message appears, it must be measured against Christ’s teaching; he cites Paul’s warning that even an angel from heaven preaching another gospel must be rejected. Respect for angelic minds therefore does not become credulity. John combines a real angelology with strict epistemic discipline.

10. The mind of God: divine Wisdom, not a creaturely psychology

John’s doctrine of God is not a theory of a large human-like mind. God does not have faculties in the way humans do. God is not one psychological subject alongside other psychological subjects. Yet John’s God is not impersonal. God knows, loves, communicates, guides, wounds, heals, illumines, and transforms.

The divine “mind,” if one uses that phrase, must be treated analogically. God is infinite Wisdom and Love. Divine wisdom is not a concept within God; it is God’s own self-knowing intelligibility. When this wisdom touches the human understanding, it appears as darkness because it exceeds finite cognitive form. The Dark Night says the same divine wisdom that purges and illumines the soul also purges and illumines angels, flowing through celestial hierarchies.

In the Living Flame, the Trinitarian structure of transformation is especially explicit. John says the living flame communicates divine knowledge to the intellect, love to the will, and delight to the substance and memory of the soul; he also says the Trinity inhabits the soul by illuminating the intellect in the wisdom of the Son, delighting the will in the Holy Spirit, and absorbing the soul in the Father’s sweetness. Benedict XVI’s Vatican audience on John summarizes this same point: true union of love culminates in Trinitarian union, and in the supreme state the soul knows everything in God rather than reaching God through creatures.

This implies a strong metaphysical claim: the highest human cognition is not merely cognition of God but cognition in God. John’s transformed soul does not simply add more propositions about divine things. It is drawn into a participatory mode in which God becomes guide, medium, and term.

The Spiritual Canticle makes this directness clear. In the solitude of perfect detachment, God guides the understanding, moves the will, and fills the memory. John then says that in spiritual marriage God works directly and immediately in the soul, not through angels or natural capacities. This is crucial for divine mind: God is not merely known at the end of a chain of representations. God can become the immediate agent of the soul’s transformed operations.

11. Angels and angelic cognition

John’s angelology belongs inside, not outside, his philosophy of mind. Angels represent finite, non-embodied, spiritual intelligence. They also mediate divine illumination and protection, though they are surpassed in the highest divine-human union.

In the Spiritual Canticle, John identifies angels as part of the “rational creation” that serves God intelligently. Angels serve by contemplation and fruition in heaven; human beings serve by loving and longing on earth. Angels also teach interiorly by secret inspirations, while human beings teach exteriorly through Scripture and doctrine. In another stanza, he says angels carry prayers to God, bring graces to souls, communicate divine inspirations, and defend against evil spirits.

John also draws on the traditional distinction between angelic “morning” and “evening” knowledge: morning knowledge is knowing created things in the Divine Word, while evening knowledge is knowing them from created things themselves. This is philosophically rich. It suggests two modes of cognition: knowledge from below, through creatures, and knowledge from above, through the Word. Human cognition ordinarily begins from below through sense; angelic and beatified cognition can know creatures in God.

Yet angels remain finite. John says God is strange not only to human beings but even to holy angels and souls who see Him, because they will never exhaust God’s judgments and wonders. Only to God is God not strange or new. This is a disciplined angelology: angels are real spiritual intellects, but not divine. They can mediate, inspire, and illuminate, but they cannot comprehend God exhaustively.

The Dark Night adds another layer. Divine wisdom illumines angels through hierarchies and then human beings; angels receive this more easily because they are pure spirits, while human beings receive it painfully because of impurity, embodiment, and the limitations of sense. But once purified, the human soul can receive in a way more analogous to angels. This yields a comparative philosophy of cognition: human minds are embodied and image-dependent at the natural level; angelic minds are spiritual and less encumbered; both remain finite before God; both depend on divine illumination.

Finally, John insists that the highest spiritual marriage is immediate divine action, not angelic mediation. Thus angels are real but not ultimate. Their place in his philosophy of mind is mediatory and comparative, not final.

12. Transformation, not annihilation

John’s language of nothingness, emptiness, darkness, and negation can mislead. He does not want the destruction of the soul’s powers. He wants their purification and transformation.

The Ascent explicitly distinguishes mystical union from pantheistic identity. God remains God and the soul remains soul; union is union of likeness, love, conformity, and participation, not essential fusion. This is vital. The soul’s faculties are not dissolved into God as if individuality disappeared. Rather, their disordered possessiveness is removed so they can be moved more perfectly.

The Living Flame gives the mature picture: the same flame that once wounded and purged the soul becomes gentle, luminous, and delightful. The purgative flame wounds by destroying bad habits; the transformed flame becomes divine light for the intellect, strength for the will, and delight for the soul’s substance. The final state is not cognitive blankness but intensified, divinized operation.

This gives a precise philosophical formula: John’s “negation” is ordered toward non-possessive plenitude. The mind is emptied of finite objects as ultimate possessions so that it can be filled by God. The will is emptied of disordered affection so that it can love more wholly. Memory is emptied of possessive images so that it can be filled with divine knowledge. Understanding is darkened as to natural light so that it can receive a higher light.

13. Systematic extraction: a Sanjuanist philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from John could be organized around the following theses.

First, the embodied-receptive thesis: natural human cognition begins through the senses and internal images. The soul is not born with ordinary natural knowledge already formed; it receives through embodied channels.

Second, the faculty-structure thesis: the human soul-mind operates through distinguishable powers: external senses, internal senses, imagination/fancy, appetite, passions, understanding, memory, and will. The higher powers are especially important for spiritual transformation.

Third, the affective-governance thesis: the will governs the passions and desires, and disordered desire distorts cognition. The mind’s access to truth depends partly on the purification of love.

Fourth, the proportionality thesis: a cognitive means must be proportionate to its object. Created forms and concepts are proportionate to creatures but not to God as infinite. Therefore the highest knowledge of God cannot be achieved by natural representation alone.

Fifth, the apophatic-cognitive thesis: divine light appears as darkness to finite understanding, not because God lacks intelligibility, but because God exceeds natural modes of intelligibility.

Sixth, the theological-virtue thesis: faith, hope, and charity are not merely moral states; they transform understanding, memory, and will. Faith darkens and elevates understanding; hope empties and opens memory; charity purifies and conforms will.

Seventh, the anti-possessive thesis: the chief obstacle to union is not creaturely existence but possessive attachment. The soul must cease treating images, consolations, memories, concepts, and spiritual experiences as possessions.

Eighth, the non-discursive-contemplation thesis: the highest earthly cognition of God is a loving, general, non-discursive attentiveness that surpasses meditation without despising meditation.

Ninth, the passivity-as-receptivity thesis: the decisive transformation of the mind is not self-produced. Active effort prepares; passive divine action completes. Passivity here means receptive availability to a higher agency, not inertia.

Tenth, the transformation-not-destruction thesis: purgation does not abolish the faculties. God perfects nature; in union, natural and moral actions become more rightly ordered.

Eleventh, the first-person-opacity thesis: the subject may not transparently know its own deepest spiritual condition. The dark night can involve real faith, hope, and love under the felt absence or obscurity of those very virtues.

Twelfth, the mental-privacy thesis: human beings do not naturally know the inward thoughts of others; they infer from signs unless God gives supernatural illumination, which itself requires caution.

Thirteenth, the angelic-comparison thesis: angelic minds show that spiritual cognition need not be embodied or image-dependent in the human way, but angels remain finite and dependent on divine illumination.

Fourteenth, the divine-hyperintelligence thesis: God’s “mind” is not a creaturely mind but infinite Wisdom and Love, communicating itself to intellect, will, memory, and the substance of the soul.

Fifteenth, the participatory-knowledge thesis: the perfected mind does not merely know more objects; it knows differently, by participation in God, in whom it can know creatures without passing through them as proximate means.

Sixteenth, the discernment thesis: extraordinary cognition is not self-validating. Claims of visions, locutions, angelic messages, or supernatural knowledge must be subordinated to Christ, faith, humility, and spiritual discernment.

14. Relation to modern philosophy of mind

John’s view is not reducible to any modern school, but it can converse fruitfully with several.

Against a narrow Cartesian internalism, John denies that the deepest operations of the soul are always transparent to introspection. The dark night shows that one may be spiritually alive while feeling dead, faithful while feeling faithless, transformed while unable to interpret the transformation. This makes him surprisingly relevant to debates about first-person authority and self-knowledge.

Against reductive materialism, John insists on spiritual faculties and divine transformation. Yet he is not indifferent to embodiment. His account of natural knowledge through sense and his remark about the brain as the seat of memory’s disturbance show that he does not treat the soul as a disembodied ghost floating free of bodily conditions.

Against purely representational theories of mind, John argues that the most important cognitive relation is not representation but loving participation. To know God most deeply is not to possess a clearer mental picture but to be transformed by divine light and love.

Against purely cognitivist accounts of belief, John gives affect and will a constitutive role. What the soul loves shapes what it can know. Disordered appetite darkens understanding; purified love opens the soul to truth.

Against purely voluntarist accounts, John also insists that love without truth is unsafe. The understanding must be purified by faith, not abandoned into fantasy. The soul must beware curiosity, false revelations, self-love, melancholy, demonic deception, and the misuse of extraordinary experiences.

The closest modern analogues are therefore not one school but several: virtue epistemology, affective cognition, phenomenology of religious experience, embodied cognition, anti-representationalism, and externalist epistemology. But John’s own synthesis is more radical because it places all of these within a metaphysics of grace and union.

15. Multilingual reception: what non-English sources add

The Spanish reception is especially valuable on anthropology and memory. González Suárez reads John’s alma as a philosophical anthropology of the human person, including lower and higher parts, external and internal senses, and the operations of understanding, will, and memory in relation to loving union. Wilhelmsen foregrounds the unresolved debate over whether memory is a truly distinct rational power or a function of intellect, which is essential for any rigorous reconstruction of John’s philosophy of mind. Pessis García and Alvarado Marambio bring John into contemporary philosophy of religion by using the dark night to challenge internalist and non-cognitivist theories of faith.

The French reception represented by Carmel en France is useful for seeing John’s psychology as an ordered whole: imagination, irascible and concupiscible tendencies, the three higher powers, and the passions all have to be directed toward their proper objects in the journey toward union. That emphasis helps prevent an overly intellectualized reading of John.

The Italian reception, especially the discussion of Cristina Campo, María Zambrano, Simone Weil, and Agamben, highlights attention, receptivity, negation, and the darkening function of the theological virtues. This is not a replacement for close reading of John, but it draws out a feature sometimes softened in English devotional reception: John’s path is not simply interior consolation but radical dispossession of the faculties’ claim to possess their objects.

16. Final synthesis

A rigorous philosophy of mind extracted from St. John of the Cross would be a theological faculty psychology of transformative cognition.

It would say that the human mind is naturally embodied, image-dependent, affectively governed, and structured by understanding, memory, and will. It would say that the mind’s disorders are not merely informational errors but disordered loves, possessive memories, restless appetites, and the attempt to reduce God to finite forms. It would say that the highest act of mind is not conceptual mastery but loving, receptive participation in divine Wisdom. It would say that faith, hope, and charity are not external religious additions to cognition but the very means by which understanding, memory, and will are purified and elevated.

It would also include God and angels as integral to the theory. God is infinite Wisdom and Love, not a creaturely mind, but the source and final term of all cognition. Angels are finite spiritual intellects who illuminate, inspire, and mediate, while remaining unable to exhaust God. Human beings, because embodied, begin below angels in cognitive immediacy; yet by purification and divine union they can be drawn into a mode of knowing in God that surpasses ordinary creaturely mediation.

The result is not a psychology of escape from mind, but a psychology of mind’s transfiguration. John’s “darkness” is not the death of intelligence. It is the painful passage from possessive cognition to participatory wisdom.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
